steps.title: Identify the slope of line l using perpendicularity
steps.content: When two lines are perpendicular, their slopes multiply to $-1$. Since line $k$ has slope $m$, line $l$ has slope $-\frac{1}{m}$.

steps.title: Use slope as change in y
steps.content:
All answer choices have $x=3$. Moving from $(2,9)$ to $x=3$ is a run of $1$ unit to the right. With slope $-\frac{1}{m}$, the $y$-value changes by:
$$1\left(-\frac{1}{m}\right)=-\frac{1}{m}$$

steps.title: Find the point
steps.content:
Start at $y=9$ and subtract $\frac{1}{m}$:
$$y=9-\frac{1}{m}$$
So the point is $\left(3,9-\frac{1}{m}\right)$.

steps.title: Evaluate all choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — '$(3, 9 - \frac{1}{m})$': Moving one unit right from $(2,9)$ along a line of slope $-\frac{1}{m}$ gives $y = 9 - \frac{1}{m}$. ✓
  • Choice B — '$(3, 9 + \frac{1}{m})$': This would require slope $+\frac{1}{m}$, but the perpendicular slope is $-\frac{1}{m}$ (negative). ✗
  • Choice C — '$(3, 9 - m)$': Moving one unit right would require slope $-m$. For this to equal the perpendicular slope $-\frac{1}{m}$ we would need $-m = -\frac{1}{m}$, i.e. $m^2 = 1$, contradicting $m > 1$. ✗
  • Choice D — '$(3, 9 + m)$': Moving one unit right would require slope $+m$. This is positive, whereas the perpendicular slope $-\frac{1}{m}$ is negative, so it cannot be correct. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
